questions.prompt: Complete the zero conditional: "If you heat ice, it ___."
questions.explanation: The zero conditional describes a general truth, so both clauses use the present simple: If you heat ice, it melts. "will melt" and "would melt" belong to the first and second conditionals, and "melted" is the past simple, which is not used here.

questions.prompt: Complete the first conditional: "If it rains tomorrow, I ___ at home."
questions.explanation: The first conditional talks about a real future result, so the result clause uses will + base verb: I will stay at home. "stay" alone is the present simple, "stayed" is past, and "would stay" is the second conditional (imaginary), not a real plan.

questions.prompt: Complete the second conditional: "If I won the lottery, I ___ the world."
questions.explanation: Winning the lottery is imaginary, so this is the second conditional: if + past simple (won), then would + base verb in the result — I would travel. "will travel" is the real-future first conditional, and "travel/travelled" leave out the modal would.

options.text: If it will rain, I will take an umbrella.
If it would rain, I will take an umbrella.
If it rains, I take will an umbrella.
If it rains, I will take an umbrella.

questions.explanation: In the first conditional the if-clause stays in the present simple and only the result uses will: If it rains, I will take an umbrella. Options (a) and (b) wrongly put will/would inside the if-clause, and (c) breaks the word order of will + take.

questions.prompt: When the if-clause comes first, what do we put between the two clauses? "If you study hard ___ you will pass."
questions.explanation: When the if-clause comes first, we separate it from the result with a comma: If you study hard, you will pass. A full stop would cut the sentence in two, "then" is optional (not required), and leaving out the comma is a punctuation mistake here.
